title: A Balls-and-Bins Model of Trade
abstract: Many of the facts about the extensive margin of trade—which firms export, and how many products are sent to how many destinations— are consistent with a surprisingly large class of trade models because of the sparse nature of trade data. We propose a statistical model to account for sparsity, formalizing the assignment of trade shipments to country, product, and firm categories as balls falling into bins. The balls-and-bins model quantitatively reproduces the pattern of zero product- and firm-level trade flows across export destinations, and the frequency of multiproduct, multidestination exporters. In contrast, balls-and-bins overpredicts the fraction of exporting firms. ( JEL F11, F14)
summary_en: Trade data is extraordinarily sparse: most firms export to only a handful of destinations, and most products are shipped by a small number of firms. This paper shows that many common "facts" about the extensive margin of trade — which firms export, to where, how many products — can be explained by a simple statistical model that assigns trade shipments to country–product–firm bins like balls falling into urns. The result is humbling: facts cited as evidence for sophisticated trade models may be consistent with pure randomness, and careful researchers need a statistical baseline before claiming structural evidence.
data_used: US Census Bureau's Linked/Longitudinal Firm Trade Transactions Database, 2002 — universe of US export shipments by firm, destination country, and 6-digit HS product code; ~20 million shipments.
policy_relevance: Cautionary note for trade-policy modelling at the Ministry of Economy and KSH: quantitative claims about the composition of Hungarian exports (numbers of destinations, products per firm) should be compared against a balls-and-bins null before they are attributed to firm heterogeneity or trade frictions. Does not change first-order trade policy, but disciplines how evidence is read. US setting, but the statistical point generalises.
